title: Singularities in Mixed Characteristic via Alterations
speaker: Karl Schwede
abstract: Multiplier ideals and test ideals are ways to measure singularities in characteristic zero and p > 0 respectively.  In characteristic zero, multiplier ideals are computed by a sufficiently large blowup by comparing the canonical module of the base and the resolution.  In characteristic p > 0, test ideals were originally defined via Frobenius, but under moderate hypotheses, can be computed via a sufficiently large alteration again via canonical modules.
tags: Special Year Workshop on p-adic Arithmetic Geometry

proof_stages.label: Pairs and rational boundary divisors
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a normal excellent integral scheme X with dualizing complex, introduces singularities of pairs (X, Delta), and explains why q-Cartier Q-divisors naturally enter through birational pullback.

proof_stages.label: Alteration-splitting definition of KLT in characteristic zero
proof_stages.summary: Under a quasi-Gorenstein simplification, KLT is formulated by requiring the map O_X to R pi_* O_Y(pi^*Delta) to split for regular alterations making pi^*Delta Cartier, strengthening the alteration-splitting criterion for rational singularities.

proof_stages.label: Multiplier ideal as the dual image
proof_stages.summary: Dualizing the KLT splitting map and using characteristic-zero vanishing identifies a resolution-independent image ideal J(O_X,Delta), the multiplier ideal, whose equality to O_X detects KLT and which is unchanged by sufficiently small Cartier perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-p alteration images and stabilized test ideals
proof_stages.summary: In characteristic p the same alteration image can be formed but depends on the cover, so the correct invariant is the descending intersection over finite covers or alterations, which stabilizes and equals the test ideal from tight closure theory.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius computation with perturbation terms
proof_stages.summary: The classical characteristic-p definition is recovered by restricting to Frobenius maps only after adding a small discriminant or test-element perturbation, yielding the same stabilized test ideal and the same right-continuity under small boundary changes.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-characteristic stabilized alteration theorem
proof_stages.summary: For schemes over a complete mixed-characteristic DVR, finite maps and birational maps alone are insufficient, but after adding an arbitrarily small multiple of a sufficiently large discriminant divisor G, the intersection of alteration images stabilizes to a single alteration image independently of G.

proof_stages.label: Identification with local perfectoid test ideals
proof_stages.summary: The stabilized mixed-characteristic alteration ideal, after completion at a point of the p-fiber, is identified with the previously defined perfectoid big-Cohen-Macaulay test ideal, so it inherits the expected formal properties of multiplier and test ideals.

proof_stages.label: Bhatt-Lurie p-adic Riemann-Hilbert mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof passes to a deeply ramified p-power-root extension, applies the Bhatt-Lurie p-adic Riemann-Hilbert functor to obtain an almost sheaf, traces it down and approximates it by alterations, thereby proving stabilization and recovering the usual multiplier ideal after inverting p.

examples.name: Quadric cone ruling with half-exceptional pullback
examples.note: A ruling on a quadric cone is non-Cartier while twice it is Cartier, and its pullback to the blow-up of the cone point acquires a one-half exceptional component, concretely showing why rational divisor coefficients arise.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely applies the blow-up/pullback construction to a quadric cone ruling and identifies the resulting half exceptional component.

passes.reason: A quadric cone is a singular/degenerate quadric, and the ruling is used to show integral pullbacks can fail by producing a half-exceptional coefficient.
A quadric cone is a singular rank-deficient quadric, and its ruling is used to show that pullback of a non-Cartier divisor can force fractional coefficients.
the singular quadric cone ruling is a non-Cartier Weil divisor whose pullback acquires a half-exceptional component, showing integral pullbacks are insufficient
A rank-deficient quadric cone with a non-Cartier ruling is a degenerate quadric used to show an integral divisor pullback can acquire a fractional exceptional coefficient.
A quadric cone is a singular rank-deficient/limiting quadric, and the ruling is used only to illustrate fractional pullbacks of non-Cartier divisors.
A rank-deficient quadric cone/ruling is used to show that pulling back an integral non-Cartier Weil divisor can acquire a half exceptional coefficient.
a singular rank-deficient quadric cone ruling is used to show that pulling back a non-Cartier Q-Cartier divisor can force a fractional exceptional coefficient
Used to refute integral pullbacks since a ruling on the singular quadric cone pulls back with a half exceptional component.
rank-deficient quadric cone with a non-Cartier ruling is used to show integral pullbacks can acquire a half exceptional coefficient
Used to show integral pullbacks fail for non-Cartier divisors, with a singular quadric cone giving a degenerate quadric case.
used to show that non-Cartier divisor pullback can force fractional coefficients, and the quadric cone is a singular degenerate quadric.
A singular rank-deficient quadric cone is used to show that pulling back a non-Cartier ruling can force a fractional exceptional coefficient, so integral divisors do not suffice.

examples.name: Complex plane cusp multiplier ideals
examples.note: For the cusp divisor V(y^2-x^3) in A^2_C, the multiplier ideal is described as the coefficient t varies, illustrating KLT thresholds, jumping numbers, and small-perturbation behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes the multiplier ideals for the cusp in A^2 over C as the coefficient t varies, giving the jumping values and ideals.

passes.reason: The plane cusp is a standard non-exceptional plane curve singularity used illustratively to compute multiplier ideals and their jump at 5/6.
The plane cusp is a standard A2 curve singularity used illustratively to compute multiplier ideals and jumping numbers.
the plane cusp y^2=x^3 is a singular degeneration used illustratively to compute multiplier-ideal jumps
The plane cusp is a standard A2 plane-curve singularity used for a multiplier-ideal computation rather than to refute a claim.
The plane cusp is a standard nonexceptional plane-curve singularity, used illustratively to compute multiplier ideals and jumping numbers.
The ordinary plane cusp is used as a standard computation of multiplier-ideal jumping, not to refute a claim.
the ordinary plane cusp is used as a standard computation of multiplier ideals and jumping behavior, not to refute a claim
A standard multiplier-ideal computation for the singular cuspidal plane curve rather than an adversarial example.
ordinary plane cusp is used as a standard computation of multiplier-ideal jumping rather than to refute a claim
Used as a standard illustration of multiplier-ideal jumps, and the cusp is a singular degeneration of a smooth plane curve.
used as a standard illustrative computation of multiplier ideals for the plane cusp, with no structural sporadic or extremal status invoked.
The plane cusp is a standard A2 curve singularity used illustratively to compute multiplier ideals and jumping behavior.

examples.name: E8 singularity in characteristic two
examples.note: The F_2 Du Val E8 singularity has one regular resolution giving image O_X but finite covers giving the maximal ideal, a counterexample that motivates replacing a single alteration image by an intersection over all covers or alterations.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the characteristic-two E8 singularity as an example where different alterations give different images, but only states the outcomes without working them out.

passes.reason: The E8 Du Val singularity is an exceptional ADE singularity and is used to show the alteration image is not independent of the chosen cover in characteristic p.
The E8 surface singularity is an exceptional ADE type and is used to show that the characteristic-zero independence from the chosen alteration fails in characteristic p.
the type-E8 Du Val surface singularity is an exceptional ADE singularity used to show alteration images need not be determined by one resolution in characteristic two
The E8 rational double point is an exceptional ADE singularity used to show the alteration image is not independent of the chosen alteration in characteristic p.
The E8 surface singularity is an exceptional ADE type, and it is used to show alteration-independence of the image formula fails in characteristic p.
The E8 Du Val singularity is an exceptional ADE case and is used to show alteration images need not be independent in characteristic p.
the E8 Du Val singularity is an exceptional ADE case and is used to show alteration-dependent images in characteristic p need not agree
Used to show alteration images need not be independent in characteristic p, with E8 an exceptional ADE surface singularity.
ADE-exceptional E8 surface singularity in characteristic two is used to show one resolution does not determine the characteristic-p image
Used to show alteration-independence fails in characteristic p, and E8 is an exceptional ADE surface singularity.
used to show independence of the alteration fails in characteristic p, and E8 is an exceptional ADE singularity type.
The sporadic E8 rational double point is used to show that in characteristic p the alteration-dependent image need not be determined by a resolution.

examples.name: Characteristic-p cusp test ideals
examples.note: The same cusp V(y^2-x^3) in characteristic p has test-ideal jumps depending on p modulo 6, illustrating how characteristic-p test ideals approximate but do not exactly equal characteristic-zero multiplier ideals.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the characteristic-p cusp case by giving the test-ideal behavior and jump values depending on p modulo 6 and on p=2,3.

passes.reason: The characteristic-p cusp is structurally a standard cusp, but its p-dependent test-ideal jumps show the naive characteristic-zero formula need not hold.
The characteristic-p cusp computation is used to show that the test-ideal formula depends on p and need not match the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal behavior.
the characteristic-p cusp is a singular plane curve used to show test-ideal jumps can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula
The characteristic-p cusp is the ordinary plane cusp used to show test-ideal jumping can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula.
The characteristic-p cusp is the ordinary cusp again, used to show the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula does not carry over exactly and depends on p.
The characteristic-p plane cusp is used to show test ideals and jumping numbers can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula.
the characteristic-p cusp computation is used to show the test-ideal jumps can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula and depend on p
Used to show characteristic-p test ideals differ from the characteristic-zero cusp formula for the singular cuspidal divisor.
positive-characteristic cusp computation is used to show test-ideal jumps depend on p and can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier ideal
Used to show characteristic-p cusp test ideals need not match the characteristic-zero formula, and the cusp is a degenerate singular plane curve.
used to show the characteristic-p test-ideal behavior differs from the characteristic-zero cusp formula, while the cusp itself is not sporadic or extremal here.
The same cusp in characteristic p is used to show that test-ideal jumps can differ from the characteristic-zero multiplier-ideal formula and depend on p.
